IX

I

As the month of August moved toward an end there was no longer any doubt as to the ‚Äúfailing‚Äù of Aunt Cassie; it was confirmed by the very silence with which she surrounded the state of her health. For forty years one had discussed Aunt Cassie‚Äôs health as one discussed the weather‚ÅÝ‚Äîa thing ever present in the consciousness of man about which one could do nothing, and now Aunt Cassie ceased suddenly to speak of her health at all. She even abandoned her habit of going about on foot and took to making her round of calls in the rattling motor which she protested to fear and loathe, and she came to lean more and more heavily upon the robust Miss Peavey for companionship and support. Claiming a fear of burglars, she had Miss Peavey‚Äôs bed moved into the room next to hers and kept the door open between. She developed, Olivia discovered, an almost morbid terror of being left alone.

And so the depression of another illness came to add its weight to the burden of Jack‚Äôs death and the grief of John Pentland. The task of battling the cloud of melancholy which hung over the old house grew more and more heavy upon Olivia‚Äôs shoulders. Anson remained as usual indifferent to any changes in the life about him, living really in the past among all the sheaves of musty papers, a man not so much cold-blooded as bloodless, for there was nothing active nor calculating in his nature, but only a great inertia, a lack of all fire. And it was impossible to turn to Sabine, who in an odd way seemed as cold and detached as Anson; she appeared to stand at a little distance, waiting, watching them all, even Olivia herself. And it was of course unthinkable to cloud the happiness of Sybil by going to her for support.

There was at least O‚ÄôHara, who came more and more frequently to Pentlands, now that the first visit had been made and the ice was broken. Anson encountered him once in the hallway, coldly; and he had become very friendly with old John Pentland. The two had a common interest in horses and dogs and cattle, and O‚ÄôHara, born in the Boston slums and knowing very little on any of these subjects, perhaps found the old gentleman a valuable source of information. He told Olivia, ‚ÄúI wouldn‚Äôt come to the house except for you. I can‚Äôt bear to think of you there‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ always alone‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ always troubled.‚Äù

And in the evenings, while they played bridge or listened to Jean‚Äôs music, she sometimes caught his eye, watching her with the old admiration, telling her that he was ready to support her no matter what happened.

A week after the encounter with Miss Peavey at the catnip-bed, Peters came to Olivia‚Äôs room late in the afternoon to say, with a curious blend of respect and confidence, ‚ÄúHe‚Äôs ill again, Mrs.¬ÝPentland.‚Äù

She knew what Peters meant; it was a kind of code between them.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ The same words used so many times before.

She went quickly to the tall narrow library that smelled of dogs and apples and woodsmoke, knowing well enough what she would find there; and on opening the door she saw him at once, lying asleep in the big leather chair. The faint odor of whisky‚ÅÝ‚Äîa smell which had come long since to fill her always with a kind of horror‚ÅÝ‚Äîhung in the air, and on the mahogany desk stood three bottles, each nearly emptied. He slept quietly, one arm flung across his chest, the other hanging to the floor, where the bony fingers rested limply against the Turkey-red carpet. There was something childlike in the peace which enveloped him. It seemed to Olivia that he was even free now of the troubles which long ago had left their mark in the harsh, bitter lines of the old face. The lines were gone, melted away somehow, drowned in the immense quiet of this artificial death. It was only thus, perhaps, that he slept quietly, untroubled by dreams. It was only thus that he ever escaped.

Standing in the doorway she watched him for a time, quietly, and then, turning, she said to Peters, ‚ÄúWill you tell Higgins?‚Äù and entering the door she closed the red-plush curtains, shutting out the late afternoon sunlight.

Higgins came, as he had done so many times before, to lock the door and sit there in the room, even sleeping on the worn leather divan, until John Pentland, wakening slowly and looking about in a dazed way, discovered his groom sitting in the same room, polishing a bridle or a pair of riding-boots. The little man was never idle. Something deep inside him demanded action: he must always be doing something. And so, after these melancholy occasions, a new odor clung to the library for days‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ the fresh, clean, healthy odor of leather and harness-soap.

For two days Higgins stayed in the library, leaving it only for meals, and for two days the old lady in the north wing went unvisited. Save for this single room, there was no evidence of any change in the order of life at Pentlands. Jean, in ignorance of what had happened, came in the evenings to play. But Sabine knew; and Aunt Cassie, who never asked questions concerning the mysterious absence of her brother lest she be told the truth. Anson, as usual, noticed nothing. The only real change lay in a sudden display of sulking and ill-temper on the part of Miss Egan. The invincible nurse even quarreled with the cook, and was uncivil to Olivia, who thought, ‚ÄúWhat next is to happen? I shall be forced to look for a new nurse.‚Äù

On the evening of the third day, just after dinner, Higgins opened the door and went in search of Olivia.

‚ÄúThe old gentleman is all right again,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúHe‚Äôs gone to bathe and he‚Äôd like to see you in the library in half an hour.‚Äù

She found him there, seated by the big mahogany desk, bathed and spotlessly neat in clean linen; but he looked very old and weary, and beneath the tan of the leathery face there was a pallor which gave him a yellowish look. It was his habit never to refer in any way to these sad occasions, to behave always as if he had only been away for a day or two and wanted to hear what had happened during his absence.

Looking up at her, he said gravely, ‚ÄúI wanted to speak to you, Olivia. You weren‚Äôt busy, were you? I didn‚Äôt disturb you?‚Äù

‚ÄúNo,‚Äù she said. ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs nothing.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Jean and Th√©r√®se are here with Sybil.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ That‚Äôs all.‚Äù

‚ÄúSybil,‚Äù he repeated. ‚ÄúSybil.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ She‚Äôs very happy these days, isn‚Äôt she?‚Äù Olivia nodded and even smiled a little, in a warm, understanding way, so that he added, ‚ÄúWell, we mustn‚Äôt spoil her happiness. We mustn‚Äôt allow anything to happen to it.‚Äù

A light came into the eyes of Olivia. ‚ÄúNo; we mustn‚Äôt,‚Äù she repeated, and then, ‚ÄúShe‚Äôs a clever girl.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ She knows what she wants from life, and that‚Äôs the whole secret. Most people never know until it‚Äôs too late.‚Äù

A silence followed this speech, so eloquent, so full of unsaid things, that Olivia grew uneasy.

‚ÄúI wanted to talk to you about‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù he hesitated for a moment, and she saw that beneath the edge of the table his hands were clenched so violently that the bony knuckles showed through the brown skin. ‚ÄúI wanted to talk to you about a great many things.‚Äù He stirred and added abruptly, ‚ÄúFirst of all, there‚Äôs my will.‚Äù

He opened the desk and took out a packet of papers, separating them carefully into little piles before he spoke again. There was a weariness in all his movements. ‚ÄúI‚Äôve made some changes,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúchanges that you ought to know about‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ and there are one or two other things.‚Äù He looked at her from under the fierce, shaggy eyebrows. ‚ÄúYou see, I haven‚Äôt long to live. I‚Äôve no reason to expect to live forever and I want to leave things in perfect order, as they have always been.‚Äù

To Olivia, sitting in silence, the conversation became suddenly painful. With each word she felt a wall rising about her, shutting her in, while the old man went on and on with an agonizing calmness, with an air of being certain that his will would be obeyed in death as it had always been in life.

‚ÄúTo begin with, you will all be left very rich‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ very rich‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ something over six million dollars. And it‚Äôs solid money, Olivia‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ money not made by gambling, but money that‚Äôs been saved and multiplied by careful living. For seventy-five years it‚Äôs been the tradition of the family to live on the income of its income. We‚Äôve managed to do it somehow, and in the end we‚Äôre rich‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ very rich.‚Äù

As he talked he kept fingering the papers nervously, placing them in neat little piles, arranging and rearranging them.

‚ÄúAnd, as you know, Olivia, the money has been kept in a way so that the principal could never be spent. Sybil‚Äôs grandchildren will be able to touch some of it‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ that is, if you are unwise enough to leave it to them that way.‚Äù

Olivia looked up suddenly. ‚ÄúBut why me? What have I to do with it?‚Äù

‚ÄúThat‚Äôs what I‚Äôm coming to, Olivia dear.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ It‚Äôs because I‚Äôm leaving control of the whole fortune to you.‚Äù

Suddenly, fiercely, she wanted none of it. She had a quick, passionate desire to seize all the neatly piled papers and burn them, to tear them into small bits and fling them out of the window.

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt want it!‚Äù she said. ‚ÄúWhy should you leave it to me? I‚Äôm rich myself. I don‚Äôt want it! I‚Äôm not a Pentland.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ It‚Äôs not my money. I‚Äôve nothing to do with it.‚Äù In spite of herself, there was a note of passionate resentment in her voice.

The shaggy brows raised faintly in a look of surprise.

‚ÄúTo whom, if not to you?‚Äù he asked.

After a moment, she said, ‚ÄúWhy, Anson‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ to Anson, I suppose.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou don‚Äôt really think that?‚Äù

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs his money‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Pentland money‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ not mine. I‚Äôve all the money I need and more.‚Äù

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs yours, Olivia.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù He looked at her sharply. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre more a Pentland than Anson, in spite of blood‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ in spite of name. You‚Äôre more a Pentland than any of them. It‚Äôs your money by every right in spite of anything you can do.‚Äù

(‚ÄúBut Anson isn‚Äôt a Pentland, nor you either,‚Äù thought Olivia.)

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs you who are dependable, who are careful, who are honorable, Olivia. You‚Äôre the strong one. When I die, you‚Äôll be the head of the family.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Surely, you know that‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ already.‚Äù

(‚ÄúI,‚Äù thought Olivia, ‚ÄúI who have been so giddy, who am planning to betray you all.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ I am all this!‚Äù)

‚ÄúIf I left it to Anson, it would be wasted, lost on foolish ideas. He‚Äôs no idea of business.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ There‚Äôs a screw loose in Anson.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ He‚Äôs a crank. He‚Äôd be giving away this good money to missionaries and queer committees‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ societies for meddling in the affairs of people. That wasn‚Äôt what this fortune was made for. No, I won‚Äôt have Pentland money squandered like that.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd I,‚Äù asked Olivia. ‚ÄúHow do you know what I will do with it?‚Äù

He smiled softly, affectionately. ‚ÄúI know what you‚Äôll do with it, because I know you, Olivia, my dear.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ You‚Äôll keep it safe and intact.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ You‚Äôre the Pentland of the family. You weren‚Äôt when you came here, but you are now. I mean that you belong to the grand tradition of Pentlands‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ the old ones who hang out there in the hall. You‚Äôre the only one left‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ for Sybil is too young. She‚Äôs only a child‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ yet.‚Äù

Olivia was silent, but beneath the silence there ran a torrent of cold, rebellious thoughts. Being a Pentland, then, was not a matter of blood: it was an idea, even an ideal. She thought fiercely, ‚ÄúI‚Äôm not a Pentland. I‚Äôm alive. I am myself. I‚Äôve not been absorbed into nothing. All these years haven‚Äôt changed me so much. They haven‚Äôt made me into a Pentland.‚Äù But for the sake of her affection, she could say none of these things. She only said, ‚ÄúHow do you know what I‚Äôll do with it? How do you know that I mightn‚Äôt squander it extravagantly‚ÅÝ‚Äîor‚ÅÝ‚Äîor even run away, taking all that was free with me. No one could stop me‚ÅÝ‚Äîno one.‚Äù

He only repeated what he had said before, saying it more slowly this time, as if to impress her. ‚ÄúI know what you‚Äôll do with it, Olivia, because I know you, Olivia dear‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou‚Äôd never do anything foolish or shameful‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI know that‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat‚Äôs why I trust you.‚Äù

And when she did not answer him, he asked, ‚ÄúYou will accept it, won‚Äôt you, Olivia? You‚Äôll have the help of a good lawyer‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ one of the best‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ John Mannering. It will please me, Olivia, and it will let the world know what I think of you, what you have been to me all these years‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ all that Anson has never been‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ nor my own sister, Cassie.‚Äù He leaned across the table, touching her white hand gently. ‚ÄúYou will, Olivia?‚Äù

It was impossible to refuse, impossible even to protest any further, impossible to say that in this very moment she wanted only to run away, to escape, to leave them all forever, now that Sybil was safe. Looking away, she said in a low voice, ‚ÄúYes.‚Äù

It was impossible to desert him now‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ an old, tired man. The bond between them was too strong; it had existed for too long, since that first day she had come to Pentlands as Anson‚Äôs bride and known that it was the father and not the son whom she respected. In a way, he had imposed upon her something of his own rugged, patriarchal strength. It seemed to her that she had been caught when she meant most to escape; and she was frightened, too, by the echoing thought that perhaps she had become, after all, a Pentland‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ hard, cautious, unadventurous and a little bitter, one for whom there was no fire or glamour in life, one who worshiped a harsh, changeable, invisible goddess called Duty. She kept thinking of Sabine‚Äôs bitter remark about ‚Äúthe lower middle-class virtues of the Pentlands‚Äù‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ the lack of fire, the lack of splendor, of gallantry. And yet this fierce old man was gallant, in an odd fashion.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Even Sabine knew that.

He was talking again. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs not only money that‚Äôs been left to you.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ There‚Äôs Sybil, who‚Äôs still too young to be let free.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúNo,‚Äù said Olivia with a quiet stubbornness, ‚Äúshe‚Äôs not too young. She‚Äôs to do as she pleases. I‚Äôve tried to make her wiser than I was at her age‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ perhaps wiser than I‚Äôve ever been‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ even now.‚Äù

‚ÄúPerhaps you‚Äôre right, my dear. You have been so many times‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ and things aren‚Äôt the same as they were in my day‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ certainly not with young girls.‚Äù

He took up the papers again, fussing over them in a curious, nervous way, very unlike his usual firm, unrelenting manner. She had a flash of insight which told her that he was behaving thus because he wanted to avoid looking at her. She hated confidences and she was afraid now that he was about to tell her things she preferred never to hear. She hated confidences and yet she seemed to be a person who attracted them always.

‚ÄúAnd leaving Sybil out of it,‚Äù he continued, ‚Äúthere‚Äôs queer old Miss Haddon in Durham whom, as you know, we‚Äôve taken care of for years; and there‚Äôs Cassie, who‚Äôs growing old and ill, I think. We can‚Äôt leave her to half-witted Miss Peavey. I know my sister Cassie has been a burden to you.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ She‚Äôs been a burden to me, all my life.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù He smiled grimly. ‚ÄúI suppose you know that.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù Then, after a pause, he said, ‚ÄúBut most of all, there‚Äôs my wife.‚Äù

His voice assumed a queer, unnatural quality, from which all feeling had been removed. It became like the voices of deaf persons who never hear the sounds they make.

‚ÄúI can‚Äôt leave her alone,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúAlone‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ with no one to care for her save a paid nurse. I couldn‚Äôt die and know that there‚Äôs no one to think of her‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ save that wretched, efficient Miss Egan‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ a stranger. No, Olivia‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ there‚Äôs no one but you.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ No one I can trust.‚Äù He looked at her sharply. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôll promise me to keep her here always‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ never to let them send her away? You‚Äôll promise?‚Äù

Again she was caught. ‚ÄúOf course,‚Äù she said. ‚ÄúOf course I‚Äôll promise you that.‚Äù What else was she to say?

‚ÄúBecause,‚Äù he added, looking away from her once more, ‚Äúbecause I owe her that‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ even after I‚Äôm dead. I couldn‚Äôt rest if she were shut up somewhere‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ among strangers. You see‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ once‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ once.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù He broke off sharply, as if what he had been about to say was unbearable.

With Olivia the sense of uneasiness changed into actual terror. She wanted to cry out, ‚ÄúStop!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Don‚Äôt go on!‚Äù But some instinct told her that he meant to go on and on to the very end, painfully, despite anything she could do.

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs odd,‚Äù he was saying quite calmly, ‚Äúbut there seem to be only women left‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ no men‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ for Anson is really an old woman.‚Äù

Quietly, firmly, with the air of a man before a confessor, speaking almost as if she were invisible, impersonal, a creature who was a kind of machine, he went on, ‚ÄúAnd of course, Horace Pentland is dead, so we needn‚Äôt think of him any longer.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ But there‚Äôs Mrs.¬ÝSoames.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù He coughed and began again to weave the gaunt bony fingers in and out, as if what he had to say were drawn from the depth of his soul with a great agony. ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs Mrs.¬ÝSoames,‚Äù he repeated. ‚ÄúI know that you understand about her, Olivia‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ and I‚Äôm grateful to you for having been kind and human where none of the others would have been. I fancy we‚Äôve given Beacon Hill and Commonwealth Avenue subject for conversation for thirty years‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ but I don‚Äôt care about that. They‚Äôve watched us‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ they‚Äôve known every time I went up the steps of her brownstone house‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ the very hour I arrived and the hour I left. They have eyes, in our world, Olivia, even in the backs of their heads. You must remember that, my dear. They watch you‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ they see everything you do. They almost know what you think‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ and when they don‚Äôt know, they make it up. That‚Äôs one of the signs of a sick, decaying world‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ that they get their living vicariously‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ by watching someone else live‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ that they live always in the past. That‚Äôs the only reason I ever felt sorry for Horace Pentland‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ the only reason that I had sympathy for him. It was cruel that he should have been born in such a place.‚Äù

The bitterness ran like acid through all the speech, through the very timbre of his voice. It burned in the fierce black eyes where the fire was not yet dead. Olivia believed that she was seeing him now for the first time, in his fullness, with nothing concealed. And as she listened, the old cloud of mystery that had always hidden him from her began to clear away like the fog lifting from the marshes in the early morning. She saw him now as he really was‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ a man fiercely masculine, bitter, clearheaded, and more human than the rest of them, who had never before betrayed himself even for an instant.

‚ÄúBut about Mrs.¬ÝSoames.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ If anything should happen to me, Olivia‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ if I should die first, I want you to be kind to her‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ for my sake and for hers. She‚Äôs been patient and good to me for so long.‚Äù The bitterness seemed to flow away a little now, leaving only a kindling warmth in its place. ‚ÄúShe‚Äôs been good to me.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ She‚Äôs always understood, Olivia, even before you came here to help me. You and she, Olivia, have made life worth living for me. She‚Äôs been patient‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ more patient than you know. Sometimes I must have made life for her a hell on earth‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ but she‚Äôs always been there, waiting, full of gentleness and sympathy. She‚Äôs been ill most of the time you‚Äôve known her‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ old and ill. You can‚Äôt imagine how beautiful she once was.‚Äù

‚ÄúI know,‚Äù said Olivia softly. ‚ÄúI remember seeing her when I first came to Pentlands‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ and Sabine has told me.‚Äù

The name of Sabine appeared to rouse him suddenly. He sat up very straight and said, ‚ÄúDon‚Äôt trust Sabine too far, Olivia. She belongs to us, after all. She‚Äôs very like my sister Cassie‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ more like her than you can imagine. It‚Äôs why they hate each other so. She‚Äôs Cassie turned inside out, as you might say. They‚Äôd both sacrifice everything for the sake of stirring up some trouble or calamity that would interest them. They live‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ vicariously.‚Äù

Olivia would have interrupted him, defending Sabine and telling of the one real thing that had happened to her‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ the tragic love for her husband; she would have told him of all the abrupt, incoherent confidences Sabine had made her; but the old man gave her no chance. It seemed suddenly that he had become possessed, fiercely intent upon pouring out to her all the dark things he had kept hidden for so long.

(She kept thinking, ‚ÄúWhy must I know all these things? Why must I take up the burden? Why was it that I should find those letters which had lain safe and hidden for so long?‚Äù)

He was talking again quietly, the bony fingers weaving in and out their nervous futile pattern. ‚ÄúYou see, Olivia.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ You see, she takes drugs now‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ and there‚Äôs no use in trying to cure her. She‚Äôs old now, and it doesn‚Äôt really matter. It‚Äôs not as if she were young with all her life before her.‚Äù

Almost without thinking, Olivia answered, ‚ÄúI know that.‚Äù

He looked up quickly. ‚ÄúKnow it?‚Äù he asked sharply. ‚ÄúHow could you know it?‚Äù

‚ÄúSabine told me.‚Äù

The head bowed again. ‚ÄúOh, Sabine! Of course! She‚Äôs dangerous. She knows far too much of the world. She‚Äôs known too many strange people.‚Äù And then he repeated again what he had said months ago after the ball. ‚ÄúShe ought never to have come back here.‚Äù

Into the midst of the strange, disjointed conversation there came presently the sound of music drifting toward them from the distant drawing-room. John Pentland, who was a little deaf, did not hear it at first, but after a little time he sat up, listening, and turning toward her, asked, ‚ÄúIs that Sybil‚Äôs young man?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes.‚Äù

‚ÄúHe‚Äôs a nice boy, isn‚Äôt he?‚Äù

‚ÄúA very nice boy.‚Äù

After a silence he asked, ‚ÄúWhat‚Äôs the name of the thing he‚Äôs playing?‚Äù

II

She did not go to her own room, because it would have been impossible to sleep, and she could not go to the drawing-room to face, in the mood which held her captive, such young faces as those of Jean and Th√©r√®se and Sybil. At the moment she could not bear the thought of any enclosed place, of a room or even a place covered by a roof which shut out the open sky. She had need of the air and that healing sense of freedom and oblivion which the sight of the marshes and the sea sometimes brought to her. She wanted to breathe deeply the fresh salty atmosphere, to run, to escape somewhere. Indeed, for a moment she succumbed to a sense of panic, as she had done on the other hot night when O‚ÄôHara followed her into the garden.

She went out across the terrace and, wandering aimlessly, found herself presently moving beneath the trees in the direction of the marshes and the sea. This last night of August was hot and clear save for the faint, blue-white mist that always hung above the lower meadows. There had been times in the past when the thought of crossing the lonely meadows, of wandering the shadowed lanes in the darkness, had frightened her, but tonight such an adventure seemed only restful and quiet, perhaps because she believed that she could encounter there nothing more terrible than the confidences of John Pentland. She was acutely aware, as she had been on that other evening, of the breathless beauty of the night, of the velvety shadows along the hedges and ditches, of the brilliance of the stars, of the distant foaming white line of the sea and the rich, fertile odor of the pastures and marshes.

And presently, when she had grown a little more calm, she tried to bring some order out of the chaos that filled her body and spirit. It seemed to her that all life had become hopelessly muddled and confused. She was aware in some way, almost without knowing why, that the old man had tricked her, turning her will easily to his own desires, changing all the prospect of the future. She had known always that he was strong and in his way invincible, but until tonight she had never known the full greatness of his strength‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ how relentless, even how unscrupulous he could be; for he had been unscrupulous, unfair, in the way he had used every weapon at hand‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ every sentiment, every memory‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ to achieve his will. There had been no fierce struggle in the open; it was far more subtle than that. He had subdued her without her knowing it, aided perhaps by all that dark force which had the power of changing them all‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ even the children of Savina Dalgedo and Toby Cane into ‚ÄúPentlands.‚Äù

Thinking bitterly of what had passed, she came to see that his strength rested upon the foundation of his virtue, his rightness. One could say‚ÅÝ‚Äîindeed, one could believe it as one believed that the sun had risen yesterday‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat all his life had been tragically foolish and quixotic, fantastically devoted to the hard, uncompromising ideal of what a Pentland ought to be; and yet‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ yet one knew that he had been right, even perhaps heroic; one respected his uncompromising strength. He had made a wreck of his own happiness and driven poor old Mrs.¬ÝSoames to seek peace in the Nirvana of drugs; and yet for her, he was the whole of life: she lived only for him. This code of his was hard, cruel, inhuman, sacrificing everything to its observance.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ ‚ÄúEven,‚Äù thought Olivia, ‚Äúto sacrificing me along with himself. But I will not be sacrificed. I will escape!‚Äù

And after a long time she began to see slowly what it was that lay at the bottom of the iron power he had over people, the strength which none of them had been able to resist. It was a simple thing‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ simply that he believed, passionately, relentlessly, as those first Puritans had done.

The others all about her did not matter. Not one of them had any power over her‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ not Anson, nor Aunt Cassie, nor Sabine, nor Bishop Smallwood. None of them played any part in the course of her life. They did not matter. She had no fear of them; rather they seemed to her now fussy and pitiful.

Stumbling along half-blindly, she found herself presently at the bridge where the lane from Pentlands crossed the river on its way to Brook Cottage. Since she had been a little girl the sight of water had exerted a strange spell upon her‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ the sight of a river, a lake, but most of all the open sea; she had always been drawn toward these things like a bit of iron toward a magnet; and now, finding herself at the bridge, she halted, and stood looking over the stone parapet in the shadow of the hawthorn-bushes that grew close to the water‚Äôs edge, down on the dark, still pool below her. The water was black and in it the bright little stars glittered like diamonds scattered over its surface. The warm, rich odor of cattle filled the air, touched by the faint, ghostly perfume of the last white nympheas that bordered the pool.

And while she stood there, bathed in the stillness of the dark solitude, she began to understand a little what had really passed between them in the room smelling of whisky and saddle-soap. She saw how the whole tragedy of John Pentland and his life had been born of the stupidity, the ignorance, the hypocrisy of others, and she saw, too, that he was beyond all doubt the grandson of the Toby Cane who had written those wild passionate letters glorifying the flesh; only John Pentland had found himself caught in the prison of that other terrible thing‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe code in which he had been trained, in which he believed. She saw now that it was not strange that he sought escape from reality by shutting himself in and drinking himself into a stupor. He had been caught, tragically, between those two powerful forces. She kept seeing him as he said, ‚ÄúI have never been unfaithful to her, not once in all the years since our wedding-night.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ I wanted you to know because, you see, you and Mrs.¬ÝSoames are the only ones who matter to me‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ and she knows that it is true.‚Äù

It seemed to her that this fidelity was a terrible, a wicked, thing.

And she came to understand that through all their talk together, the thought, the idea, of Michael had been always present. It was almost as if they had been speaking all the while about Michael and herself. A dozen times the old man had touched upon it, vaguely but surely. She had no doubts that Aunt Cassie had long since learned all there was to learn from Miss Peavey of the encounter by the catnip-bed, and she was certain that she had taken the information to her brother. And yet, as she looked back upon her talk with the old man, it seemed to her that in a dozen ways, by words, by intonation, by glances, he had implied that he knew the secret. Even in the end when, cruelly, he had with an uncanny sureness touched the one fear, the one suspicion that marred her love for Michael, by saying in the most casual way, ‚ÄúStill, I think we‚Äôd better be careful of him. And then the most fantastic of all thoughts occurred to her‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ that all their talk together, even the painful, tragic confidence made with such an heroic effort, was directed at herself. Standing there on the bridge in the black shadow of the hawthorns, all sense of time or space, of the world about her, faded out of existence, so that she was aware of herself only as a creature who was suffering. In the midst of this rebellion, she became aware, with that strange acuteness which seemed to touch all her senses, that she was no longer alone on the bridge in the midst of empty, mist-veiled meadows. She knew suddenly and with a curious certainty that there were others somewhere near her in the darkness, perhaps watching her, and she had for a moment a wave of the quick, chilling fear which sometimes overtook her at Pentlands at the times when she had a sense of figures surrounding her who could neither be seen nor touched.
